content:
Modern **support vector machines** emerged from **Vladimir Vapnik** and colleagues' work on **statistical learning theory** and **structural risk minimization** in the **1960s–1990s** at the **Institute of Control Sciences** in Moscow.

The **hard-margin SVM** (1992) formalized **max-margin** linear classification for separable data. **Boser, Guyon, and Vapnik** (1992) introduced the **kernel trick**, extending margins to nonlinear feature spaces — detailed in **Kernels**.

**Cortes and Vapnik** (1995) published the **soft-margin** formulation with slack variables and penalty **C**, making SVMs practical on noisy, non-separable datasets — the **C** parameter defined canonically in this topic.

**Sequential Minimal Optimization (SMO)** by **Platt** (1998) and efficient implementations (**LIBSVM**, **LIBLINEAR**) made large-scale training feasible.

SVMs dominated **text classification**, **bioinformatics**, and **computer vision** (HOG detectors) in the **2000s** before deep learning surged; they remain strong baselines for **high-dimensional sparse** tabular and text problems.

timeline.label: Statistical learning theory (1960s–70s)
timeline.description: Vapnik and Chervonenkis develop VC theory linking capacity control to generalization — foundation for margin-based learning.

timeline.label: Hard-margin SVM (1992)
timeline.description: Max-margin hyperplane for linearly separable data; support vectors identified as points with active constraints.

timeline.label: Soft-margin SVM (1995)
timeline.description: Cortes & Vapnik add slack ξᵢ and penalty C — canonical soft-margin trade-off taught in this topic.

timeline.label: SMO & LIBSVM (late 1990s)
timeline.description: Practical solvers scale training; SVMs become standard tools in NLP and vision before deep learning era.
